Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings to listen to lecture recordings since I learn best by hearing things, but life kept getting in the way. I have a lot of extracurricular commitments, and sometimes I'd realize days had passed without opening my course materials. I wanted to be more consistent, but I just couldn't find a rhythm that stuck.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense cramming sessions right before deadlines. I kept telling myself I'd establish regular study times, but between my activities and trying to manage everything else, it never really happened. Deadlines were basically my only consistent motivator.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or seeing a discussion post pop up in my email that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me to check in more because earning points or badges gave me a little dopamine hit. But without those external prompts, I'd forget the LMS existed for days.
Q4: There was a brief period early in the semester when I tried to make checking the LMS part of my morning routine, but it didn't last long. The gamified elements helped for a while because I liked seeing my progress on the leaderboard and collecting achievements. But once I fell behind on assignments, logging in started to feel discouraging rather than motivating. It went from being a habit to something I actively avoided because it reminded me how behind I was.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could listen to them while doing other things or replay confusing parts. I also found the discussion boards useful since hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click for me in a way that just reading never did. The gamified tasks were engaging too because they broke up the monotony and made learning feel less like a chore. Anything audio-based or interactive worked well for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and PDFs because staring at text on a screen just doesn't work for me. I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read them. The quizzes I often delayed until the last minute because they stressed me out, even though I knew practicing would help. If something didn't have audio or wasn't immediately engaging, it just sat there untouched in my downloads folder.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would actually help me understand something or if it gave me immediate feedback on how I was doing. The gamified elements made even simple tasks feel more rewarding because there was a sense of accomplishment. I also valued anything that felt efficient, like if I could learn something in 20 minutes instead of reading for an hour. If it felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time, even though some of them were actually interesting. Required activities came with guilt and anxiety attached, which sometimes made me avoid them even more. With optional stuff, there was no pressure, so ironically I sometimes engaged with those more genuinely when I did look at them. But mostly, optional things got sacrificed when I was scrambling to complete what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded almost all the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also had a study group where we'd meet in person and quiz each other verbally, which really helped me retain information. I used YouTube videos and podcasts on related topics when the course material wasn't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with headphones in, nowhere near the LMS.
Q10: There were so many times I'd be walking around campus listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, really trying to absorb the material. Or I'd be in my study group for hours discussing concepts and testing each other out loud. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best studying happened through conversation and audio, but the LMS only saw me as barely present because I wasn't clicking around on the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, maybe watch a few minutes online, then listen to the whole thing multiple times offline over the next week. The LMS probably showed me accessing it once for like ten minutes, but I actually spent hours with that content. Same with any materials I could convert to audio or have read aloud to me. My one-time download was really the beginning of my engagement, not the end.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was dealing with some personal stuff that made me feel sad and unmotivated, and my attendance dropped even more than usual. I'd look at the pile of incomplete assignments and just feel overwhelmed and paralyzed. The anxiety of being behind made it harder to start anything, which made me more behind, which made me more anxious. It was a vicious cycle I couldn't break out of.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from my extracurriculars or dealing with competing responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Confusion about what was actually due or how assignments connected to each other made me avoid logging in entirely sometimes. When I was fatigued, I'd tell myself I'd just check things tomorrow, and then tomorrow would become next week. My LMS usage was basically a barometer of how overwhelmed I was feeling.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open and technically be watching a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes and absorbed nothing. Sometimes I'd click through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Being logged in and being mentally present were two very different things for me.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was perfectionism, like I wanted to do them well so I kept waiting for the 'right' time when I'd have enough energy and focus. Part of it was just avoidance because starting felt overwhelming. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just scared of failing and putting it off made me feel like I had more time to somehow figure it out.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 24 hours before something was due. I'd be completely absent for days, then suddenly I'm logging in five times in one night trying to finish everything. It wasn't healthy or effective, but without that pressure, I'd just keep pushing things off indefinitely. The deadline was my external structure since I couldn't create internal structure myself.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging or showed me I was on the right track, it motivated me to engage more with the next thing. But when feedback was critical or I got a bad grade, I'd sometimes avoid the course for days because I felt ashamed. The gamified feedback was actually easier to handle because it felt less personal, like losing points in a game rather than failing as a person. Real feedback from instructors hit different and affected my willingness to show up.
Q18: The course structure was honestly hard for me to follow sometimes. With eight online courses, keeping track of what was due where felt impossible. When modules were clearly organized and I could see how things connected, I engaged more. But when it felt disjointed or I couldn't figure out the logic of the sequence, I'd disengage because I didn't know where to start. Structure either helped me climb or made me feel lost.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier because they gave me small wins and made progress visible. Having video content was essential for my learning style. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when the navigation was confusing. Also, having so many different platforms and courses meant the LMS was just one of many tabs I had to manage, and it often got lost in the shuffle.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's trying to figure things out, clicking around looking for what they need, but struggling to actually complete the work. Maybe they're anxious and keep checking things without being able to commit to finishing. Or they're confused about requirements and spending time trying to understand what's expected. High activity doesn't mean productive activity, it could mean someone is lost or overwhelmed.
Q21: That kind of describes me in some ways. I performed okay on assignments when I actually submitted them, my assignment scores were decent. But my LMS activity was low because I did so much studying offline through audio and group work. Someone could absolutely be learning effectively without the LMS showing it, especially if they're auditory learners or prefer studying with physical materials or other people. The platform doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, and whether you submit things. That's useful for seeing basic engagement and identifying students who've disappeared. But it completely misses the quality of engagement, whether someone's actually understanding material, and all the learning that happens offline. It can't measure if someone listened to a downloaded lecture ten times or discussed concepts for hours with classmates. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just showing up or clicking through things, it's being mentally present and invested in your own growth. For me, engagement looks like actively listening, asking questions, and trying to connect new information to what I already know. It's an internal state that doesn't always match external metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was actually struggling emotionally or how hard I was trying despite my low attendance and completion rates. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures on my phone or the genuine learning that happened in my study group. It also wouldn't show that when I did engage with the gamified tasks, I actually felt hopeful and motivated. My numbers look like I didn't care, but I cared a lot, I just couldn't translate that into consistent visible performance.
